algorithms.name: Reverse Linked List
algorithms.analogy: A chain of train cars where each car points to the next. To reverse, you walk the chain and flip each coupling. Three workers travel together — next saves where to go, curr flips its link to prev, prev records the last flipped car.
algorithms.story: Reversing a linked list is the 'hello world' of pointer manipulation. Master the three-pointer dance: prev=null, curr=head, next=curr.next. At each step: save next, flip curr's link to prev, advance both prev and curr. When curr is null, prev is the new head. The recursive version is elegant but uses O(n) stack space.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Palindrome linked list check (reverse second half, compare)
• Reversing sub-lists (K-group reversal)
• Rotating a list by K positions (3-reversal technique)
• Any in-place restructuring of a linked list
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialize prev=null, curr=head
2. While curr != null:
   a. Save next = curr.next
   b. curr.next = prev  (flip the link)
   c. prev = curr       (advance prev)
   d. curr = next       (advance curr)
3. Return prev as the new head
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Draw boxes and arrows before coding — trace [1→2→3→null] step by step on paper first
• Common follow-up: reverse only positions m to n (save the node before m, reverse m..n, reconnect)
• Edge cases: null list, single node, reversing entire list — all handled by the same code
• Recursive version: after the recursive call, head.next.next = head flips the link, head.next = null breaks the old forward link
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Palindrome Linked List","desc":"Find middle, reverse second half, compare element by element — O(n) time O(1) space (LeetCode 234)."},{"title":"Reverse Nodes in K-Groups","desc":"Repeatedly call reverse() on windows of K nodes. Foundation of LeetCode 25 — hard problem reducible to this basic reversal."},{"title":"Rotate Linked List","desc":"Rotating a list by K = reversing entire list, then reversing first K, then reversing remaining K+1..n (LeetCode 61)."},{"title":"Reorder List","desc":"Find middle, reverse second half, then interleave first and reversed second half — LeetCode 143."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting to save next before overwriting curr.next — causes the rest of the list to be lost","Returning curr instead of prev at the end — curr is null when the loop exits","Recursive version: forgetting head.next = null — creates a cycle in the list","Not handling null or single-node input"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Reverse K-Group","desc":"Reverse every K consecutive nodes. If remaining nodes < K, leave them as-is. LeetCode 25."},{"name":"Reverse Between m and n","desc":"Reverse only the sublist from position m to n in one pass. LeetCode 92."},{"name":"Palindrome Check via Reversal","desc":"Split at mid, reverse second half, compare — O(n) time O(1) space. LeetCode 234."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Three-pointer dance: prev=null, curr=head. Loop: save next, flip curr.next=prev, advance prev=curr, curr=next. When curr is null, prev is the new head. NEVER return curr at the end — it's null when the loop exits. This operation is the atomic unit of all complex linked list restructuring.
algorithms.patternSignal: reverse linked list, palindrome linked list, K-group reversal, rotate list, reorder list, reverse between m and n
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Reverse Linked List","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Three-pointer: prev=null, curr=head. Save next, flip, advance — return prev"},{"name":"Reverse Linked List II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Find node before position m, reverse m..n segment, reconnect"},{"name":"Reverse Nodes in K-Group","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Count K nodes ahead. If enough, reverse group and recurse on remainder"},{"name":"Palindrome Linked List","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Find middle, reverse second half, compare, restore"},{"name":"Reorder List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Find middle, reverse second half, interleave first and reversed second"}]

algorithms.name: Floyd's Cycle Detection
algorithms.analogy: Two runners on a circular track. The tortoise runs 1 step/second, the hare runs 2. If there is a loop, the hare will lap the tortoise — they MUST meet inside the cycle. If there is no loop, the hare reaches null first.
algorithms.story: Floyd's algorithm detects cycles in O(1) space — no HashSet needed. Phase 1 detects: slow and fast meet inside the cycle. Phase 2 finds the entry: reset slow to head, advance both one step at a time — they meet exactly at the cycle start. The proof uses modular arithmetic on cycle length.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Detect cycle in linked list
• Find cycle entry node
• Find duplicate in array (array-as-linked-list trick, LeetCode 287)
• Happy number problem (detect infinite loop)
• Detect loop in any functional sequence
algorithms.howItWorks:
Phase 1 — Detect cycle:
1. slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast != null AND fast.next != null:
   slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next
3. If fast == null → no cycle, return null
4. slow == fast means cycle exists

Phase 2 — Find entry node:
5. Reset slow = head (fast stays at meeting point)
6. Move both slow and fast ONE step at a time
7. When slow == fast → this is the cycle entry node
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Phase 2 is the hard part — explain WHY resetting slow to head and moving both at speed 1 finds the entry (the math: distance from head to entry = distance from meeting point to entry within the cycle)
• Always check fast != null AND fast.next != null before fast.next.next to avoid NullPointerException
• Do NOT check slow == fast before the first move — both start at head, they would immediately be equal
• Common variant: find duplicate in array (LeetCode 287) — treat array values as 'next pointers'
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Detect Cycle (LeetCode 141)","desc":"Classic O(1) space solution using fast and slow pointers — faster in practice than HashSet approach."},{"title":"Find Cycle Entry (LeetCode 142)","desc":"Phase 2 pinpoints the exact node where the cycle begins — used in memory leak analysis."},{"title":"Find Duplicate Number (LeetCode 287)","desc":"Treat the array as a linked list where arr[i] points to next node. The duplicate is the cycle entry."},{"title":"Happy Number (LeetCode 202)","desc":"The digit-square sequence either reaches 1 (happy) or enters a known cycle — Floyd's detects the cycle."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Checking slow==fast before moving (both start at head — immediate false positive)","Forgetting fast.next != null check — causes NPE on fast.next.next","Phase 2: forgetting to move fast too — only slow resets to head, both must advance together","Confusing cycle entry with meeting point — they are different nodes"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Find Cycle Length","desc":"After Phase 1 meeting, keep slow fixed and advance fast until it laps slow — count the steps to get cycle length."},{"name":"Find Duplicate Number","desc":"LeetCode 287: treat array indices as linked list nodes. Floyd's finds the duplicate in O(n) time O(1) space."},{"name":"Happy Number Detection","desc":"LeetCode 202: apply Floyd's to the digit-square function sequence instead of a linked list."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Phase 1 finds MEETING POINT inside cycle (not the entry point). Phase 2 resets slow to head, advances both one step at a time — they meet at CYCLE ENTRY. Do NOT check slow==fast before the first move — both start at head so they'd immediately be equal (false positive).
algorithms.patternSignal: cycle in linked list, cycle entry point, detect duplicate number, happy number, circular sequence, Floyd's algorithm, slow fast pointer cycle
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Linked List Cycle","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Classic fast/slow — fast moves 2, slow moves 1. If they meet, there's a cycle"},{"name":"Linked List Cycle II","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Phase 1: detect meeting. Phase 2: reset slow to head, advance both at speed 1 until they meet again"},{"name":"Find the Duplicate Number","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Treat array as linked list: index i points to arr[i]. Duplicate = cycle entry node"},{"name":"Happy Number","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Apply Floyd's to the digit-square sequence — unhappy numbers enter a known cycle"}]

algorithms.name: Merge Two Sorted Lists
algorithms.analogy: Two sorted card decks face-up. Compare the top card of each deck, pick the smaller one into the result pile, repeat. A dummy head node acts as a placeholder so you never need special handling for the very first card.
algorithms.story: The dummy head pattern is the key insight here. Create a dummy node before the real list starts — your current pointer starts there. Compare l1.val vs l2.val, attach the smaller node, advance that list's pointer. At the end, attach whichever list still has nodes. Return dummy.next.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Merge K sorted lists (use this as the building block)
• Merge sort on linked lists (split, sort, merge)
• Combining sorted query results or data streams
• Any problem requiring in-order merging of two sequences
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create dummy = new ListNode(0), curr = dummy
2. While l1 != null AND l2 != null:
   a. If l1.val <= l2.val: curr.next = l1; l1 = l1.next
   b. Else: curr.next = l2; l2 = l2.next
   c. curr = curr.next
3. Attach remaining: curr.next = (l1 != null) ? l1 : l2
4. Return dummy.next
algorithms.interviewTips:
• The dummy head node eliminates the special case of building the first node — use this pattern whenever building a new linked list
• Follow-up: Merge K sorted lists — use a min-heap of size K (O(n log K)) or recursive divide-and-conquer (O(n log K))
• Distinguish from merging two sorted arrays: arrays need O(m+n) extra space, lists can be done in O(1) by rewiring pointers
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Merge K Sorted Lists (LeetCode 23)","desc":"Use this as the building block: either heap-based or divide-and-conquer using pairwise merging."},{"title":"Sort Linked List (LeetCode 148)","desc":"Merge sort on linked lists: find middle, split, sort each half recursively, then merge — O(n log n) time O(1) space."},{"title":"Combine Sorted Streams","desc":"Streaming merge of sorted database query results or log files, where random access is not available."},{"title":"Merge Sorted Arrays Variant","desc":"Same algorithm concept applied to arrays — LeetCode 88 merges two sorted arrays in-place."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting curr.next = l1 != null ? l1 : l2 to attach the remaining list (only one list is empty when the loop exits)","Moving curr before setting curr.next — curr would point to the wrong place","Not using a dummy head — requires special casing for the first node","Recursive version: O(n+m) stack space — not O(1) space, important for interviews"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Merge K Sorted Lists","desc":"Use a PriorityQueue (min-heap) of size K: pop minimum, add to result, push its next. O(n log K)."},{"name":"Sort Linked List","desc":"Merge sort: find middle with fast/slow, split, recursively sort both halves, merge. O(n log n) O(1) space."},{"name":"Merge Sorted Arrays","desc":"Same two-pointer merge concept applied to arrays — LeetCode 88 merges in-place starting from the end."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Dummy head node is the key pattern — eliminates special casing for the first node. Start curr at dummy, build result list, return dummy.next. Always attach the remaining non-null list at the end with curr.next = (l1 != null) ? l1 : l2 — don't loop through it.
algorithms.patternSignal: merge sorted linked lists, merge K sorted lists, dummy head pattern, sort linked list merge sort, combine sorted streams
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Merge Two Sorted Lists","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Dummy head + two pointers. Attach remaining list at end in one line"},{"name":"Merge K Sorted Lists","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Min-heap of [val, list-idx, node-idx]. Pop minimum, push its next into heap"},{"name":"Sort List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Merge sort: find middle (split), sort halves recursively, merge — O(n log n) O(1) space"},{"name":"Merge Sorted Array","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Fill from the END to avoid overwriting — same two-pointer merge logic"}]

algorithms.name: Find Middle Node
algorithms.analogy: One person walks, another runs double speed. When the runner reaches the end, the walker is exactly at the midpoint. The fast pointer covers 2 nodes per step — so when it hits null (even length) or the last node (odd length), slow is exactly in the middle.
algorithms.story: The fast/slow pointer approach finds the middle in one pass without knowing the length. For even-length lists there are two valid midpoints — the condition you use determines which one. This pattern is the backbone of linked list merge sort and palindrome checking.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Merge sort on linked lists (need to split at mid)
• Palindrome check (split + reverse second half)
• Finding median in a linked list
• Reorder list (LeetCode 143): interleave first and reversed second half
• Delete middle node
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast != null AND fast.next != null:
   slow = slow.next
   fast = fast.next.next
3. slow is now the middle node

For even-length lists:
• fast != null condition → second of two middles (right middle)
• fast.next != null condition → first of two middles (left middle)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Clarify what 'middle' means for even-length lists before coding — first or second middle?
• For merge sort, you need to split the list: find the node BEFORE the middle (use fast.next != null && fast.next.next != null) then set slow.next = null
• Combine with reversal for palindrome check — a classic two-algorithm chaining problem that demonstrates O(1) space thinking
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Palindrome Linked List (LeetCode 234)","desc":"Find middle, reverse second half, compare first half with reversed second. O(n) time O(1) space."},{"title":"Reorder List (LeetCode 143)","desc":"Split at middle, reverse second half, interleave nodes from both halves — all three steps use patterns built on find-middle."},{"title":"Merge Sort on Linked List","desc":"Recursively split list at middle, sort each half, merge. findMiddle is called O(log n) times during the split phase."},{"title":"Delete Middle Node (LeetCode 2095)","desc":"Find the node just before the middle using a slight variant, then unlink the middle node."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Off-by-one for even lists: fast != null gives right middle, fast.next != null gives left middle — know which you need","For merge sort split: must set slow.next = null to actually break the list into two separate halves","Starting fast at head.next instead of head — changes which middle you get and can cause errors"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Split at Left Middle (for merge sort)","desc":"Use fast.next != null && fast.next.next != null to get the left middle, then slow.next = null splits the list."},{"name":"Palindrome Linked List","desc":"Find middle, reverse second half, compare both halves — O(n) time O(1) space."},{"name":"Reorder List","desc":"Find middle, reverse second half, merge alternate nodes from both halves. LeetCode 143."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: fast != null gives RIGHT (second) middle for even-length lists. fast.next != null gives LEFT (first) middle needed for merge sort split (so you can set slow.next = null to split). Always clarify which middle you need before coding — it changes the while condition.
algorithms.patternSignal: find middle linked list, fast slow pointers, split list, palindrome check, merge sort linked list, middle node
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Middle of the Linked List","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"fast != null && fast.next != null — slow lands at right middle for even lists"},{"name":"Reorder List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Find middle, reverse second half, interleave the two halves"},{"name":"Delete the Middle Node of a Linked List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Use fast.next!=null && fast.next.next!=null — slow lands just BEFORE middle"},{"name":"Palindrome Linked List","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Find middle, reverse second half, compare, restore"}]

algorithms.name: Remove Nth Node From End
algorithms.analogy: Two runners start together. Runner A gets a head start of N steps. Then both run at the same speed. When Runner A reaches the end, Runner B is exactly N steps behind — at the predecessor of the node to delete. Use a dummy head so deleting the first node needs no special case.
algorithms.story: The N-gap two-pointer technique eliminates the need for a second pass. Advance the fast pointer N+1 steps ahead (not N — because slow starts at the dummy node, one before head). Then advance both until fast is null. slow.next is the target — set slow.next = slow.next.next to remove it.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Remove Nth node from end (single pass)
• Find Kth node from end
• Split list at Kth position from end
• Any 'measure from the end without knowing length' problem
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create dummy node; dummy.next = head
2. slow = dummy, fast = dummy
3. Advance fast N+1 steps (not N — slow is at dummy, not head)
4. While fast != null:
   slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next
5. slow.next = slow.next.next  (unlink the Nth from end)
6. Return dummy.next
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Always use a dummy head when you might need to delete the first node (n equals list length)
• The advance is N+1 (not N) because slow starts at dummy — one step before head
• Trace through [1→2→3→4→5] n=2: fast advances 3 steps to node 3, then both walk until fast=null — slow ends at node 3, deletes node 4 ✓
• This same pattern (gap of N) works for 'find Kth from end' with no modification
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Remove Nth From End (LeetCode 19)","desc":"Single-pass O(n) solution using N+1 gap between two pointers — classic two-pointer problem."},{"title":"Find Kth From End","desc":"Same N-gap trick but return slow instead of deleting slow.next — used in interview warm-up questions."},{"title":"Delete Middle Node (LeetCode 2095)","desc":"Find the node just before the middle using a variant of the gap technique, then unlink the middle."},{"title":"Split List at K From End","desc":"Use the Nth-from-end pointer as the split point to divide a list into two sub-lists."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Advancing fast N steps instead of N+1 — slow ends up AT the node to delete, not the predecessor","Not using a dummy head — when n equals list length, head must be deleted and the code breaks without dummy","Off-by-one in the advance loop: <= n vs < n depends on where slow starts (dummy vs head)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Find Kth Node From End","desc":"Same N-gap technique but return slow.next instead of deleting it — O(n) single pass."},{"name":"Remove Middle Node","desc":"Use fast/slow pointers where fast moves 2x — when fast reaches end, slow is just before middle."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Advance fast N+1 steps (not N) because slow starts at dummy (one before head). When fast=null, slow is at the predecessor of the node to delete. Always use dummy head — without it, deleting head (n == list length) requires special handling that breaks the uniform pattern.
algorithms.patternSignal: remove Nth from end, Kth from end, gap technique, one-pass without knowing length, N-distance two pointers
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Remove Nth Node From End of List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Dummy head + advance fast N+1 steps. When fast=null, slow is at predecessor of node to delete"},{"name":"Find Kth Node From End","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Same N-gap but advance fast N steps (not N+1) since slow starts at head not dummy"},{"name":"Delete the Middle Node","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Use fast/slow: when fast hits end, slow is just before middle — set slow.next = slow.next.next"},{"name":"Swapping Nodes in a Linked List","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Find Kth from start and Kth from end using gap technique, then swap values"}]
